sections.section_label: II. Blocs' Relationship to the Settlement

indicators.name: Cohesiveness of CLB
Cohesiveness of LB
indicators.question: For each period and bloc, please state how cohesive/fragmented the bloc was.
indicators.responses: 1. The bloc was very incohesive, with extreme competition among different factions or fractions. 2. The bloc was fairly incohesive: it had different factions or fractions, which were very competitive. 3. The bloc was fairly cohesive: it had different factions or fractions, which were moderately competitive. 4. The bloc was very cohesive and had no major competing factions.

indicators.name: Cohesiveness of OB
Hierarchical power concentration of CLB
Hierarchical power concentration of LB
indicators.question: For each period and bloc, please state, on average, how powerful high-level leaders were vis a vis intermediate-level leaders and ordinary members/ followers. Clariﬁcation: Note, to be powerful here implies an ability to dictate terms to other actors, thanks to the unlikelihood of being removed, abandoned, or otherwise sanctioned by other actors. Note further that high level leaders are likely, as individuals, to have a national, or in federal states, state-level sphere of influence by virtue of their official or unofficial positions in the bloc’s most powerful sub- groups. Intermediate level leaders are likely as individuals, to have a regional or sub-regional sphere of influence by virtue of their official or unofficial positions in the bloc’s most powerful sub-groups, or perhaps a national sphere of influence but only in the bloc’s less powerful sub-groups. Ordinary members or followers do not occupy disproportionately influential official or unofficial positions in the bloc’s sub-groups and are not disproportionately influential as individuals.
indicators.responses: 1. De facto power rested with ordinary members/followers 2. De facto power was shared relatively equally across leaders and ordinary members/followers. 3. De facto power rested with intermediate level leaders and ordinary members/followers 4. De facto power rested with intermediate level leaders 5. De facto power was shared by high-level leaders and intermediate level leaders 6. De facto power rested with high-level leaders

indicators.name: Hierarchical power concentration of OB
Likelihood CLB splits from government
indicators.question: How high was the (perceptible) likelihood that the CLB (or a majority of it) would split or withdraw support from the LB? Clariﬁcation: Where withdrawing support could, for example, play out as the CLB not backing the Leader in an internal party election, or not defending the Leader in the event of a violent or other challenge from the OB.
indicators.responses: 1. High: there was a high likelihood that the CLB would split or withdraw support from the LB. 2. Medium: there was a moderate likelihood that the CLB would split or withdraw support from the LB; 28 3. Low: there was a possibility that the CLB would split or withdraw support from the LB but only a low one;

indicators.name: Likelihood OB joins government
indicators.question: How high was the (perceptible) likelihood that the OB (or a majority of it) would join the LB in the governing coalition?
indicators.responses: 1. None: there was virtually no possibility that the OB would join the LB in the governing coalition; 2. Low: there was a possibility that the OB would join the LB in the governing coalition, but a low one; 3. Medium: there was a moderate likelihood that the OB would join the LB in the governing coalition; 4. High: there was a high likelihood that the OB would join the LB in the governing coalition.

indicators.name: Percentage of relatively powerless in CLB
Percentage of relatively powerless in LB
indicators.question: For each period and bloc, please estimate what percentage of the bloc falls into this ‘relatively powerless’ category. If there are really bloc-periods (cells) without powerless groups, indicate this by writing ‘0’. Also make sure this is consistent with your answer to Q6. Clariﬁcation: Following the example above, poor women, when added to other relatively powerless groups in the bloc, might constitute 50% of the LB. As such, coders should enter ‘50’ under ‘LB’. Unlike Q1, rows should not add up to 100. They are independent from each other.

indicators.name: Percentage of relatively powerless in OB
Population share (in %) of CLB
Population share (in %) of OB
Relative power of CLB
Relative power of LB
indicators.question: Given the repressive capabilities of the Leader’s Bloc, please estimate how powerful each bloc would likely have appeared to the Leader to be. Clariﬁcation: Note we ask about perceptions because one of the things we will test is the relationship between leadership perceptions and policy commitment. As such, power that was not perceptible to the Leader or that was only perceived ex 27 post is not of interest to us. Granted, this creates some methodological difficulties as the perceptions of the Leader are not entirely transparent. However, we ask you to consider as evidence speeches, statements or policy documents by the Leader/governing coalition; commentaries by contemporary observers identifying the relative size and strength of the blocs; convincing historical accounts of the Leader/governing coalition’s mindset; etc.
indicators.responses: 1. Powerless: it would likely make virtually no difference in struggles over the settlement. 2. Somewhat powerless: it would likely make only a small difference in struggles over the settlement. 3. Somewhat powerful: it could not single-handedly change the settlement or prevent it from being changed, but would likely make a significant difference in struggles over the settlement. 4. Quite powerful: it could not single-handedly change the settlement or prevent it from being changed, but would likely make a big difference in struggles over the settlement. 5. Extremely powerful: it could single-handedly change the settlement or prevent it from being changed by others.

indicators.name: Relative power of OB

indicators.name: Non-violent repression for leaders of LB
indicators.section_label: II. Blocs' Relationship to the Settlement
indicators.question: For each period and bloc, please estimate, on average, how important non-violent repression was as a strategy by the country’s de facto leader to incorporate his and other blocs’ leaders into or under the settlement (irrespective of whether the strategy was ultimately successful). Clariﬁcation: Nonviolent repression: This refers to the containment of challenges to the settlement by means such as: legal confinement, surveillance, infiltration, tax audits, interference in the ability to gain employment or business, restrictions on 34 fund raising, negative propaganda or scapegoating, censorship, restrictions on access to the media, outlawing assembly, etc.; General note on question 10 and 11 (related variables): See “3.2.1 Violent repression for leaders of LB (q10_violrepresslead_lb)”.
indicators.responses: 1. not important 2. slightly important 3. fairly important 4. very important

indicators.section_label: II. Blocs' Relationship to the Settlement
indicators.question: For each bloc, please provide an opinion on the scale, on average, of any settlement-generated material benefits (eg salaries, rents, public spending) it received relative to its size:
indicators.responses: 1. Very High: It received a level of material benefits very disproportionately high relative to its size; 2. High: It received a level of material benefits disproportionately high relative to its size; 3. Proportionate: It received a level of material benefits about proportionate to its size; 4. Low: It received a level of material benefits disproportionately low relative to its size. 5. Very Low: It received a level of material benefits very disproportionately low relative to its size.

indicators.name: Concentration       of          policy-decision-making                        power             in          leader
Concentration       of          policy-implementation                        power              in          leader
Foreign financial and technical support importance
indicators.question: For each period, how important to the maintenance of the settlement was financial or technical assistance by a foreign power?
indicators.responses: 1. Not important: The government could maintain the settlement wholly through its own financial and technical means. 2. Marginally important: The government received some foreign financial and technical support which was helpful; however, the government would probably have managed to maintain the settlement without it. 3. Important: Without foreign financial and technical support, the settlement would probably have collapsed sooner. 4. Very important: Without foreign financial and technical support, the settlement would almost certainly have collapsed much sooner.

indicators.name: Physical     threat        by         ethnic,           regional            or         religious            groups
Physical threat by a non-neighboring country
Physical threat by an opposition group in exile
Physical threat by neighboring country
Physical threat by rural dominant classes
Physical threat by rural subordinate classes
indicators.question: For each period, was there a (perceptible) threat to the physical survival of high or intermediate-level LB leaders from rural subordinate classes? Clariﬁcation: Physical survival refers to the ability to live without fear of being killed, imprisoned or driven into exile.
indicators.responses: 5. No threat; 6. Low threat; 7. Moderate threat; 8. High threat.

indicators.name: Physical threat by the military
Physical threat by urban dominant classes
Physical threat by urban subordinate classes

Political threat by neighboring country
Political threat by rural dominant classes
Political threat by rural subordinate classes
indicators.question: For each period, was there a (perceptible) threat to the political survival of high level LB leaders from rural subordinate classes? Clariﬁcation: Political survival refers to the ability to stay in office, and ‘perceptible threat’ means ‘perceptible to those leaders’.
indicators.responses: 45 1. No threat; 2. Low threat; 3. Moderate threat; 4. High threat.

SECTION IV: FOREIGN INFLUENCE AND INTERNAL AND     EXTERNAL THREATS  3.4.1 Foreign military support importance
Manufacturing firms' political power
indicators.question: Please specify the average level of political power of domestically owned firms in the formal manufacturing sector.
indicators.responses: 1. Low: The government found it easy to dictate terms to firms. 2. Medium: The government found it neither easy nor hard to dictate terms to firms. 3. High: The government found it hard to dictate terms to firms.

indicators.name: SECTION VI: ECONOMIC ORGANIZATIONS  3.5.1 Manufacturing firms' capabilities
FDI strategy
indicators.question: Please specify how the government treated Foreign Direct Investment (FDI).
indicators.responses: 1. The government discouraged the inflow of FDI; 2. The government permitted FDI, but placed strong conditions on it and/or provided little support; 3. The government encouraged FDI, placing moderate conditions on it and/or providing moderate support; 4. The government strongly encouraged FDI, placing few conditions on it and/or providing a high level of support; 5. The government did not have an FDI strategy

indicators.name: Government's industry versus agriculture prioritization
Government's prioritization of economic development
indicators.question: Was economic development a priority for the top leadership, beyond their statements?
indicators.responses: 1. Very low priority 2. Low priority 3. Medium priority 4. High priority 5. Very high priority

indicators.name: Government's prioritization of social development
indicators.question: Was social development, for example spending on education, health, potable water, social insurance, etc, a priority for the top leadership, beyond their statements?
indicators.responses: 1. Very low priority 2. Low priority 3. Medium priority 4. High priority 50 5. Very high priority

indicators.name: Intervention of state in economy
indicators.question: Please specify how vigorously the state intervened in the economy.
indicators.responses: 49 1. Strong: The state controlled most industries and heavily regulated and coordinated private companies, or at least it attempted to. 2. Medium: The state controlled only a few key industries, yet strongly regulated and coordinated private companies, or at least it attempted to. 3. Light: The state controlled only a few key industries if any, and otherwise did not intervene strongly in the business of private enterprises.

indicators.name: SECTION VI: ECONOMIC AND SOCIAL POLICY  3.6.1 Industrialization strategy
Horizontal Power Dummy
indicators.question: To what extent is power concentrated in the governing coalition vis-à-vis the OB?
indicators.construction: Dummy of “x_horizontalpower” using mean (=3.28) as cut-off point.
indicators.scale: Dichotomous, where 1 indicates above-mean governing coalition (=horizontal) power.

indicators.name: Khanian four-category settlement types
indicators.question: To what degree is a country’s de facto power concentrated in a country’s de facto leader’s bloc (LB)? Re-creating Mushtaq Khan’s two-dimensional political power concentration typology.
indicators.construction: Combination of “x_horizontalpowerdummy” and “x_verticalpowerdummy” into four categories, illustrated as follows: x_horizontalpowerdummy 1 0 x_vertical- 1 1. Strong-Dominant 2.Vulnerable- powerdummy Authoritarianism 0 4. Weak-Dominant 3. Competitive-Clientelism
indicators.scale: Nominal, 1-4.

indicators.name: LB vs CLB power ratio
indicators.question: How powerful is the LB vis-à-vis the CLB?
indicators.construction: = q2_power_lb / q2_power_clb
indicators.scale: Ordinal, where higher values indicate greater relative LB power, 0.2-5. 52

indicators.name: Power Concentration Index dummy
indicators.question: To what degree is a country’s de facto power concentrated in a country’s de facto leader’s bloc (LB)?
indicators.construction: Dummy of “x_powerconcentration_add” using mean (=0.56) as cut-off point.
indicators.scale: Dichotomous, where 1 indicates above-mean power concentration.

indicators.name: Vertical Power Dummy
indicators.question: To what degree is power within the governing coalition concentrated in the leader of the LB (i.e. the country’s de facto leader)?
indicators.construction: Dummy of “x_verticalpower” using mean (=0.44) as cut-off point.
indicators.scale: Dichotomous, where 1 indicates above-mean vertical power concentration.

indicators.name: Vertical Power Index
indicators.question: To what degree is power within the governing coalition concentrated in the leader of the LB (i.e. the country’s de facto leader)?
indicators.construction: Principal component analysis based weighted index of four variables: o LB vs CLB power ratio (x_lbvsclbpowerratio) – Weight: 0.52 o Likelihood CLB splits from government (q3_clbsplit)Likelihood CLB splits from government (q3_clbsplit) – Weight: 0.50 o Hierarchical power concentration of LB (q8_hierarchy_lb) – Weight: 0.43 o Cohesiveness of LB (q9_cohesiveness_lb) – Weight: 0.54 Thereafter, min-max-normalized to range from 0-1.
indicators.scale: Interval, where higher values indicate greater vertical power concentration, 0-1.

indicators.name: Average cooptation score for CLB followers
indicators.question: What is the average cooptation score for CLB followers (out of the “q11_*_clb” cooptation options)?
indicators.construction: = (q11_clientmatcooptfoll_clb + q11_clientnonmatfoll_clb + q11_progmatlegitfoll_clb + q11_ideologlegitfoll_clb + q11_demlegitfoll_clb) / 5
indicators.scale: Interval, higher scores indicating a higher average cooptation score, 1-4.

indicators.name: Average cooptation score for CLB leaders
indicators.question: What is the average cooptation score for CLB leaders (out of the “q10_*_clb” cooptation options)?
indicators.construction: = (q10_clientmatcooptlead_clb + q10_clientnonmatlead_clb + q10_progmatlegitlead_clb + q10_ideologlegitlead_clb + q10_demlegitlead_clb) /5
indicators.scale: Interval, higher scores indicating a higher average cooptation score, 1-4.

indicators.name: Average cooptation score for LB followers
indicators.question: What is the average cooptation score for LB followers (out of the “q11_*_lb” cooptation options)? 59
indicators.construction: = (q11_clientmatcooptfoll_lb + q11_clientnonmatfoll_lb + q11_progmatlegitfoll_lb + q11_ideologlegitfoll_lb + q11_demlegitfoll_lb) / 5
indicators.scale: Interval, higher scores indicating a higher average cooptation score, 1-4.

indicators.name: Average cooptation score for LB leaders
indicators.question: What is the average cooptation score for LB leaders (out of the “q10_*_lb” cooptation options)?
indicators.construction: = (q10_clientmatcooptlead_lb + q10_clientnonmatlead_lb + q10_progmatlegitlead_lb + q10_ideologlegitlead_lb + q10_demlegitlead_lb) / 5
indicators.scale: Interval, higher scores indicating a higher average cooptation score, 1-4.

indicators.name: Average cooptation score for OB followers
indicators.question: What is the average cooptation score for OB followers (out of the “q11_*_ob” cooptation options)?
indicators.construction: = (q11_clientmatcooptfoll_ob + q11_clientnonmatfoll_ob + q11_progmatlegitfoll_ob + q11_ideologlegitfoll_ob + q11_demlegitfoll_ob) / 5
indicators.scale: Interval, higher scores indicating a higher average cooptation score, 1-4.

indicators.name: Average cooptation score for OB leaders
indicators.question: What is the average cooptation score for OB leaders (out of the “q10_*_ob” cooptation options)?
indicators.construction: = (q10_clientmatcooptlead_ob + q10_clientnonmatlead_ob + q10_progmatlegitlead_ob + q10_ideologlegitlead_ob + q10_demlegitlead_ob) / 5
indicators.scale: Interval, higher scores indicating a higher average cooptation score, 1-4.

indicators.name: Dummy of Share of powerful citizens in total population
indicators.question: What share of the population are relatively powerful (irrespective of the bloc they belong to)?
indicators.construction: Dummy of “x_totpowerfulpopshare” using mean (=0.48) as cut-off point.
indicators.scale: Dichotomous, where 1 indicates above-mean share of powerful people in the total population.

indicators.name: General         clientelistic               non-material                     cooptation                    score
General clientelistic material cooptation score
indicators.question: What is the general level of clientelistic material cooptation throughout society?
indicators.construction: = 0.25 * q11_clientmatcooptfoll_lb + 0.25 * q11_clientmatcooptfoll_clb + 0.5 * q11_clientmatcooptfoll_ob
indicators.scale: Interval, higher scores indicating a larger cooptation, 1-4
indicators.notes: The weights assure that the governing coalition (LB + CLB) and the OB are weighted equally. Also, we are only taking followers into account here.

indicators.name: General cooptation score
indicators.question: What is the general level of cooptation throughout society?
indicators.construction: = 0.25 * x_lbgencooptindex + 0.25 * x_clbgencooptindex + 0.5 * x_obgencooptindex
indicators.scale: Interval, higher scores indicating a larger cooptation, 0-1 65
indicators.notes: The weights assure that the governing coalition (LB + CLB) and the OB are weighted equally.

indicators.name: General non-violent repression score
indicators.question: What is the general level of non-violent repression throughout society?
indicators.construction: = 0.25 * q11_nonviolrepressfoll_clb + 0.25 * q11_nonviolrepressfoll_clb + 0.5 * q11_nonviolrepressfoll_ob
indicators.scale: Interval, higher scores indicating a larger cooptation, 1-4
indicators.notes: The weights assure that the governing coalition (LB + CLB) and the OB are weighted equally. Also, we are only taking followers into account here.

indicators.name: General procedural democratic legitimation score
General programmatic material legitimation score
General universalistic ideological legitimation score
General violent repression score
indicators.question: What is the general level of violent repression throughout society?
indicators.construction: = 0.25 * q11_violrepressfoll_lb + 0.25 * q11_violrepressfoll_clb + 0.5 * q11_violrepressfoll_ob
indicators.scale: Interval, higher scores indicating a larger cooptation, 1-4
indicators.notes: The weights assure that the governing coalition (LB + CLB) and the OB are weighted equally. Also, we are only taking followers into account here.

indicators.construction: Equals the maximum score out of the following variables: o q11_clientmatcooptfoll_clb o q11_clientnonmatfoll_clb o q11_progmatlegitfoll_clb o q11_ideologlegitfoll_clb o q11_demlegitfoll_clb
indicators.scale: Ordinal, higher scores indicating a higher “highest” cooptation score, 1-4.

indicators.name: Joined repression and cooptation score index for CLB followers
Joined repression and cooptation score index for CLB leaders
Joined repression and cooptation score index for LB followers
Joined repression and cooptation score index for LB leaders
Joined repression and cooptation score index for OB followers
Joined repression and cooptation score index for OB leaders
Overall Cooptation Index for CLB
indicators.question: What is the general cooptation level (including both repression and positive cooptation) for the CLB at large (i.e. for both leaders and followers jointly)?
indicators.construction: = 0.5 * x_clbcooptjoinedleaders + 0.5 * x_clbcooptjoinedfollowers The score is then min-max-normalized to range from 0 to 1 using the respective lowest and highest theoretical (rather than empirical) score (being 1 and 4).
indicators.scale: Interval, higher scores indicating a higher cooptation score, 0-1

indicators.name: Overall Cooptation Index for LB
indicators.question: What is the general cooptation level (including both repression and positive cooptation) for the LB at large (i.e. for both leaders and followers jointly)?
indicators.construction: = 0.5 * x_lbcooptjoinedleaders + 0.5 * x_lbcooptjoinedfollowers 62 The score is then min-max-normalized to range from 0 to 1 using the respective lowest and highest theoretical (rather than empirical) score (being 1 and 4).
indicators.scale: Interval, higher scores indicating a higher cooptation score, 0-1

indicators.name: Overall Cooptation Index for OB
indicators.question: What is the general cooptation level (including both repression and positive cooptation) for the OB at large (i.e. for both leaders and followers jointly)?
indicators.construction: = 0.5 * x_obcooptjoinedleaders + 0.5 * x_obcooptjoinedfollowers The score is then min-max-normalized to range from 0 to 1 using the respective lowest and highest theoretical (rather than empirical) score (being 1 and 4).
indicators.scale: Interval, higher scores indicating a higher cooptation score, 0-1

indicators.name: SOCIAL FOUNDATION AND COOPTATION  4.2.1 Governing Coalition Size (in %)
Share     of    powerful           supporters             of       OB          in       total         population
Share of any CLB members that are coopted
indicators.question: What percentage of the CLB members (irrespective of whether they are powerful) is coopted under the settlement?
indicators.construction: = x_clbgencooptindex * q1_populationsahre_clb
indicators.scale: Interval, higher scores indicating a larger coopted member share, 0-100

indicators.name: Share of any LB members that are coopted
indicators.question: What percentage of the LB members (irrespective of whether they are powerful) is coopted under the settlement?
indicators.construction: = x_lbgencooptindex * q1_populationsahre_lb
indicators.scale: Interval, higher scores indicating a larger coopted member share, 0-100

indicators.name: Share of any OB members that are coopted
indicators.question: What percentage of the OB members (irrespective of whether they are powerful) is coopted under the settlement?
indicators.construction: = x_obgencooptindex * q1_populationsahre_ob
indicators.scale: Interval, higher scores indicating a larger coopted member share, 0-100

indicators.name: Share of powerful CLB members that are coopted
Share of powerful LB members that are coopted
Share of powerful OB members that are coopted
Share of powerful citizens in total population
indicators.question: What share of the population are relatively powerful (irrespective of the bloc they belong to)?
indicators.construction: = x_lbpowerfulsharetotpop + x_clbpowerfulsharetotpop + x_obpowerfulsharetotpop
indicators.scale: Interval, from low to high, 0-100.

indicators.name: Share of powerful supporters of CLB
indicators.question: Which share of the CLB’s supporters are relatively powerful?
indicators.construction: = 100 – q7_powerlesspercentage_clb
indicators.scale: Interval, from low to high, 0-100.

indicators.name: Share of powerful supporters of CLB in total population
Share of powerful supporters of LB
indicators.question: Which share of the LB’s supporters are relatively powerful?
indicators.construction: = 100 – q7_powerlesspercentage_lb
indicators.scale: Interval, from low to high, 0-100.

indicators.name: Share of powerful supporters of LB in total population
Share of powerful supporters of OB
indicators.question: Which share of the OB’s supporters are relatively powerful?
indicators.construction: = 100 – q7_powerlesspercentage_ob
indicators.scale: Interval, from low to high, 0-100.

indicators.name: Social foundation size dummy
indicators.question: What share of of the population is both potentially disruptive/powerful and co-opted by the country’s leadership.
indicators.construction: Dummy of “x_socialfoundationsize” using mean (=0.41) as cut-off point.
indicators.scale: Dichotomous, where 1 indicates an above-mean social foundation size.

indicators.name: Total coopted population
indicators.question: What percentage of the population (irrespective of power) is coopted under the settlement?
indicators.construction: = x_lbanysharecoopted + x_clbanysharecoopted + x_obanysharecoopted
indicators.scale: Interval, higher scores indicating a larger coopted member share, 0-100

indicators.name: ESID Political Settlement Index (using 'x_powerconcentration_add')
ESID Political Settlement Index (using 'x_powerconcentration_add')
ESID Political Settlement Index (using 'x_powerconcentration_mix')
ESID Political Settlement Index (using 'x_powerconcentration_mix')
ESID Political Settlement Index (using 'x_powerconcentration_multi')
ESID Political Settlement Index (using 'x_powerconcentration_multi')
ESID's    four-category           settlement            types          (using          x_tppspc_add_nor)
ESID's four-category settlement types
indicators.question: To what extent is power both concentrated in the leadership of the country and the social foundation relatively large? Re-creating Kelsall et al.’s (forthcoming) two-dimensional political settlement typology.
indicators.construction: Combination of “x_sfsdummy” and “x_pcdummy” into four categories, illustrated as follows: 70 x_pcdummy 0 1 1 2. Broad-Disp. 1. Broad-Conc. x_sfsdummy 0 3. Narrow-Disp. 4. Narrow-Conc.
indicators.scale: Nominal, 1-4.

indicators.name: Horizontal distribution inequality
indicators.question: To what extent are material benefits unequally distributed in society to the benefit of the LB and the loss of the OB?
indicators.construction: = [inverted scale]q12_crossblocdistri_lb / [inverted scale]q12_crossblocdistri_ob
indicators.scale: Interval, higher scores indicating a larger cross-bloc distribution inequality (in favor of the LB), 0.25-5.

indicators.name: Highest      political        threat          from           rural         or        urban          subordinates
Highest domestic non-elite physical threat level
Highest domestic non-elite political threat level
Highest domestic physical threat level
indicators.question: What is the highest domestic physical threat score (out of the “q19_*” variables)?
indicators.construction: Equals the maximum score out of the following variables: o q19_phythreat_ruralsub o q19_phythreat_ruraldom o q19_phythreat_urbansub o q19_phythreat_urbandom o q19_phythreat_ethnoregrel o q19_phythreat_exileoppos o q19_phythreat_military
indicators.scale: Ordinal, higher scores indicating a higher “highest” physical threat score, 1-4.

indicators.name: Highest domestic political threat level
indicators.question: What is the highest domestic political threat score (out of the “q18_*” variables)?
indicators.construction: Equals the maximum score out of the following variables: o q18_polthreat_ruralsub o q18_polthreat_ruraldom o q18_polthreat_urbansub o q18_polthreat_urbandom o q18_polthreat_ethnoregrel o q18_polthreat_exileoppos o q18_polthreat_military
indicators.scale: Ordinal, higher scores indicating a higher “highest” political threat score, 1-4.

indicators.name: Highest external physical threat from exile opposition or neighbor
Highest external physical threat level
indicators.question: What is the highest external physical threat score (out of the “q19_*” variables)?
indicators.construction: Equals the maximum score out of the following variables: o q19_phythreat_neighbcntry o q19_phythreat_nonneighbcntry
indicators.scale: Ordinal, higher scores indicating a higher “highest” physical threat score, 1-4.

indicators.name: Highest external political threat level
indicators.question: What is the highest external political threat score (out of the “q18_*” variables)? 73
indicators.construction: Equals the maximum score out of the following variables: o q18_polthreat_neighbcntry o q18_polthreat_nonneighbcntry
indicators.scale: Ordinal, higher scores indicating a higher “highest” political threat score, 1-4.

indicators.name: Highest physical threat level
indicators.question: What is the highest physical threat score (out of the “q19_*” variables)?
indicators.construction: Equals the maximum score out of the following variables: o q19_phythreat_ruralsub o q19_phythreat_ruraldom o q19_phythreat_urbansub o q19_phythreat_urbandom o q19_phythreat_ethnoregrel o q19_phythreat_exileoppos o q19_phythreat_military o q19_phythreat_neighbcntry o q19_phythreat_nonneighbcntry
indicators.scale: Ordinal, higher scores indicating a higher “highest” physical threat score, 1-4.

indicators.name: Highest political threat from rural or urban subordinates or ethno/religious/reg
Highest political threat level
indicators.question: What is the highest political threat score (out of the “q18_*” variables)?
indicators.construction: Equals the maximum score out of the following variables: o q18_polthreat_ruralsub o q18_polthreat_ruraldom o q18_polthreat_urbansub o q18_polthreat_urbandom o q18_polthreat_ethnoregrel o q18_polthreat_exileoppos o q18_polthreat_military 72 o q18_polthreat_neighbcntry o q18_polthreat_nonneighbcntry
indicators.scale: Ordinal, higher scores indicating a higher “highest” political threat score, 1-4.

indicators.name: Systemic threat         (Polit.:         Non-elite; Phys.:                     exile &                neighbor)
Systemic threat (Crude and physical threat)
indicators.question: Is there a simultaneous physical threat both from inside and outside the country?
indicators.construction: = x_highdomphysthreatlvl * x_highextphysthreatlvl
indicators.scale: Ordinal, higher scores indicating a higher systemic threat, 1-16.

indicators.name: Systemic threat (Non-elite and physical threat)
indicators.question: Is there a simultaneous physical threat from a foreign nation and domestic non-elite groups?
indicators.construction: = x_highdomphysnonelthreatlvl * x_highextphysthreatlvl
indicators.scale: Ordinal, higher scores indicating a higher systemic threat, 1-16.

indicators.name: Systemic threat (Polit.: Non-elite & ethnic; Phys.: exile & neighbor)
THREATS AND FOREIGN SUPPORT  4.5.1 Joined foreign support importance index
BUSINESS CONFIGURATION  4.6.1 Business Configuration (Capability * Power)
DEVELOPMENT POLICY  4.7.1 Expert opinion whether industrial policy exists
Economic Policy Index
indicators.question: How “open” and “unrestrictive” is the economic policy of the country?
indicators.construction: Principal component analysis based weighted index of three variables: o x_industrialpolicyscore – Weight: 0.57 o x_fdistrategyscore – Weight: 0.59 o q24_stateintervention – Weight: 0.57 Thereafter, min-max-normalized to range from 0-1.
indicators.scale: Interval, where higher values indicate greater economic policy openess, 0-1.

indicators.name: Economic Policy Index dummy
indicators.question: How “open” and “unrestrictive” is the economic policy of the country?
indicators.construction: Dummy of “x_econpolicy” using mean (=0.575) as cut-off point.
indicators.scale: Dichotomous, where 1 indicates an above-mean open economic policy.

indicators.name: Expert opinion whether FDI policy exists
indicators.question: Did the country-year have an FDI policy or not?
indicators.construction: Confidence-weighted mean (see also 2.1.3) of expert code’s whether a country-year experienced an FDI policy (i.e. chosing options 1-3) or not (i.e. chosing option 4) in variable “3.6.2 FDI strategy (q23_fdistrategy)”. This way, if one expert very confidently states there was an FDI policy strategy despite two experts non-confidently stating they do not think one existed, the country-year might still have been coded to have had an FDI policy.
indicators.scale: Interval, higher scores indicate greater agreement that policy existed, 0-1.

indicators.name: Four-categorical economic policy and power concentration typology
Mixed ISI-EOI dummy
indicators.question: To what extent is industrial policy geared towards a mix of ISI and EOI rather than either of the two?
indicators.construction: Coded “1” if x_industrialpolicyyes >= 1.5 and x_industrialpolicyyes <= 2.5. Coded “0” otherwise.
indicators.scale: Dichotomous, where 1 indicates a mix of the two and 0 stands for either of the two.

indicators.name: Multiplicative economic policy and power concentration index
Multiplicative industrial policy and power concentration index
Six-categorical industrial policy and power concentration typology (1.5, 2.5)
Six-categorical industrial policy and power concentration typology (1.66, 2.33)
Three-categorical industrial policy variable
indicators.question: Was industrial policy more geared towards import-substituting or export- oriented industrialization, or a mix of the two?
indicators.construction: 0. = (ISI) x_industrialpolicyyes < 1.5 1. = (MIX) x_industrialpolicyyes >= 1.5 & < 2.5 2. = (EOI) x_industrialpolicyyes >= 2.5
indicators.scale: Ordinal, higher scores indicating a move towards a more export-oriented industrialization strategy, 0-3.

indicators.name: Three-categorical industrial policy variable
indicators.question: Was industrial policy more geared towards import-substituting or export- oriented industrialization, or a mix of the two?
indicators.construction: 0. = (ISI) x_industrialpolicyyes < 1.66 1. = (MIX) x_industrialpolicyyes >= 1.66 & < 2.33 2. = (EOI) x_industrialpolicyyes >= 2.33
indicators.scale: Ordinal, higher scores indicating a move towards a more export-oriented industrialization strategy, 0-2.
